questions.text: Simple Harmonic Motion of a body is described by which statements mentioned below:K: K.E is maximum when displacement x = 0L: P.E is maximum when x = 0M: P.E is maximum when x = ± x0
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option includes one correct and one incorrect statement. Kinetic energy is indeed maximum at displacement x = 0, but potential energy is not maximum at x = 0; it is minimum there. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
This is the correct option. Kinetic energy is maximum when displacement x = 0, and potential energy is maximum at the extreme positions (x = ± x0), not at the equilibrium position.
This option incorrectly includes the statement that potential energy is maximum at x = 0, which contradicts the true nature of simple harmonic motion. Thus, this option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect because it contains the incorrect statement that potential energy is maximum at x = 0, which is not true in simple harmonic motion.

questions.explanation: Option B is the correct choice because it accurately describes the energy conditions in simple harmonic motion. Kinetic energy (K.E) reaches its maximum when the displacement is zero (x = 0), as the body is moving fastest through the equilibrium position. Potential energy (P.E), on the other hand, is maximum at the extreme positions (x = ± x0) because these are the points of maximum displacement from the equilibrium position. Option A is incorrect because it incorrectly states that potential energy is maximum at x = 0. Option C includes the same incorrect statement as Option A. Option D is incorrect for the same reason as it includes the incorrect statement that potential energy is maximum at x = 0.

questions.text: Under the action of the restoring force:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The speed of the body always increases
options.explanation: The speed of the body always increases. This is incorrect because the restoring force is always directed towards the equilibrium position. This means that the restoring force will always act to slow down the body as it approaches its equilibrium position.

options.text: The body moves at constant speed
options.explanation: The body moves at constant speed. This is also incorrect because the restoring force will always act to change the speed of the body. The speed of the body will only be constant if the restoring force is balanced by another force, such as friction.

The body always slows down. This is incorrect because the restoring force will not always act to slow down the body. The restoring force will only act to slow down the body as it approaches its equilibrium position.

The body accelerates under the action of the restoring force. The restoring force is a force that acts to bring a body to its equilibrium position. The restoring force is always directed towards the equilibrium position, and it is proportional to the displacement of the body from its equilibrium position. When a body is displaced from its equilibrium position, the restoring force will act to bring the body back to its equilibrium position. This will cause the body to accelerate towards its equilibrium position. The acceleration of the body will be greatest when the body is farthest from its equilibrium position, and it will decrease as the body approaches its equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: Correct option is D.Under the action of the restoring force, the body accelerates because the force acts to bring the body back to its equilibrium position. This force is proportional to the displacement from equilibrium, as described by Hooke's Law (F = -kx), leading to acceleration (a = F/m = -ω^2x).Option A is incorrect because the speed does not always increase; it depends on the direction of motion relative to the equilibrium.Option B is incorrect because the speed is not constant; it varies as the body moves in response to the restoring force.Option C is incorrect because the body does not always slow down; it speeds up when moving toward the equilibrium position from a point of maximum displacement.

questions.text: The speed V of the waves in the string depends upon the tension F of the string and m, the mass per unit length of the string. it is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: V^2 = F/m: This equation suggests that the square of the wave speed (V^2) is equal to the tension (F) divided by the mass per unit length (m). Hence, this equation is correct.

It states that the wave speed (V) is equal to the tension (F) divided by the mass per unit length (m). This equation reflects the direct proportionality between wave speed and tension, while inversely proportional to the mass per unit length.However, the correct relation should either have V^2 or square root of F/m.

V * m = F: This equation suggests that the product of wave speed (V) and the mass per unit length (m) is equal to the tension (F). However, this equation is not correct. The correct equation does not involve multiplication between wave speed and mass per unit length.

V = F * m: This equation suggests that the wave speed (V) is equal to the product of tension (F) and mass per unit length (m). However, this equation is not correct. The correct equation involves dividing the tension by the mass per unit length, not multiplying them.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: V2 = F/m. This equation accurately represents the physical relationship where the square of the wave speed is determined by the tension in the string divided by the mass per unit length. This formula is derived from fundamental principles of wave mechanics. Option B is incorrect because it does not square the speed as required by the law. Option C incorrectly suggests multiplication instead of division, and Option D suggests an incorrect relationship by multiplying tension and mass per unit length, which does not apply in this context.
